title: UKPDS 80
subtitle: 10-Year Follow-up of Intensive Glucose Control — Summary, Why/How, MCQ & T/F Module
authors: Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA; N Engl J Med 2008

trialDesign.fullName: UK Prospective Diabetes Study (UKPDS) — 10-Year Post-Trial Follow-up (UKPDS 80)
trialDesign.design: Post-trial observational follow-up of randomized UKPDS glucose-control arms
trialDesign.population: 3,277 surviving UKPDS participants with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes at original enrollment
trialDesign.sampleSize: n=3277 survivors (SU-insulin vs conventional; metformin vs conventional in overweight patients)
trialDesign.duration: 10 years post-trial monitoring; median total follow-up ~16.8 person-years (SU-insulin)
trialDesign.intervention: Original randomization: intensive sulfonylurea-insulin or metformin (if >120% ideal weight) vs conventional diet
trialDesign.comparator: Conventional dietary glucose control during original trial; all returned to usual care post-trial
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: Persistence of microvascular benefit and emergence of macrovascular benefits over 10-year follow-up
trialDesign.overview: Despite HbA1c convergence within 1 year, legacy benefits persisted: microvascular ↓24%, MI and mortality reductions emerged for SU-insulin; metformin showed MI ↓33% and death ↓27%.

headings.background: UKPDS showed microvascular benefit with intensive glucose control; macrovascular benefit was uncertain at trial end.
headings.design: 10-year post-trial monitoring of 3,277 survivors; no maintenance of randomized therapies.
headings.population: Newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes at UKPDS entry; metformin arm restricted to overweight (>120% ideal weight).
headings.intervention: Original intensive SU-insulin or metformin vs conventional diet during interventional phase.
headings.comparator: Conventional therapy; post-trial all groups received community/hospital standard care.
headings.followUp: 10 years post-trial; clinic visits years 1–5; questionnaire follow-up years 6–10.

outcomes.label: Microvascular disease — SU-insulin
outcomes.interpretation: Persistent at 10 years despite glycemic convergence

safetyHighlights: No significant stroke reduction in either intensive arm
Metformin group had 15% higher mean plasma creatinine — only significant biochemical difference
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: Early intensive glucose control in newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes produces a legacy effect — enduring microvascular and macrovascular benefits even after glycemic levels converge.

inclusionCriteria: Original UKPDS: newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes; age 25–65; fasting glucose >108 mg/dL on two occasions
Metformin randomization: body weight >120% of ideal weight
exclusionCriteria: Original UKPDS exclusions: ketonuria, creatinine >2.0 mg/dL, MI in prior year, angina/heart failure, retinopathy requiring laser, severe concurrent illness
keyTakeaways: Legacy effect: HbA1c differences lost within 1 year, but benefits persist 10+ years.
SU-insulin: microvascular ↓24%; MI ↓15%; all-cause death ↓13% at 10 years.
Metformin (overweight): MI ↓33%; death ↓27%. Stroke not reduced. Early intervention matters.

items.title: UKPDS 80 — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Objective: The original UKPDS showed intensive glucose therapy reduced microvascular complications in newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes, but macrovascular benefits were not statistically significant at trial end. This 10-year post-trial monitoring study assessed whether benefits persisted and whether macrovascular advantages eventually emerged.

Methodology: 3,277 surviving patients followed for 10 years after the trial ended. Patients returned to standard care with no attempt to maintain original randomized therapies (intensive sulfonylurea-insulin, intensive metformin, or conventional diet).

Loss of Glycemic Differences: HbA1c differences between intensive and conventional groups disappeared within the first year of post-trial follow-up.

Sulfonylurea–Insulin Outcomes: Despite glycemic convergence, intensive SU-insulin maintained 24% microvascular RR reduction; MI (15%) and all-cause death (13%) reductions became statistically significant over 10 years.

Metformin Outcomes: Overweight patients originally on intensive metformin maintained profound reductions: MI 33%, all-cause death 27%.

Conclusion ("The Legacy Effect"): Early intensive glycemic control in newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes yields enduring microvascular and macrovascular benefits even when blood sugar levels converge later — the legacy effect.
keyPoints: HbA1c differences lost within 1 year · benefits persist 10+ years
items.reference: Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why was post-trial monitoring necessary after the UKPDS originally concluded in 1997?
items.answer: Post-trial monitoring was necessary to determine if the microvascular benefits observed during the trial persisted and to see if a longer timeframe would reveal a significant long-term effect on macrovascular outcomes, such as myocardial infarction.
keyPoints: Post-trial monitoring was necessary to determine if the microvascular benefits observed during the trial persisted and to see if a longer timeframe would reveal a significant long-term effect on macrovascular outcomes, such as myocardial infarction.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q1: Post-trial monitoring was necessary to determine if the microvascular benefits o... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How did the clinical management of patients change once they entered the post-trial monitoring phase?
items.answer: Patients returned to community or hospital-based diabetes care based on their clinical needs, with no attempt by researchers to maintain the previously randomized therapies (intensive or conventional).
keyPoints: Patients returned to community or hospital-based diabetes care based on their clinical needs, with no attempt by researchers to maintain the previously randomized therapies (intensive or conventional).
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q2: Patients returned to community or hospital-based diabetes care based on their cl... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why did the glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) levels between the two groups converge so quickly (within one year) after the trial ended?
items.answer: When the UKPDS results were published in 1998, both patients and clinicians were advised that lowering blood glucose as much as possible was necessary, leading standard-care patients to adopt more intensive regimens that matched the original intensive-therapy group.
keyPoints: When the UKPDS results were published in 1998, both patients and clinicians were advised that lowering blood glucose as much as possible was necessary, leading standard-care patients to adopt more intensive regimens that matched the original intensive-therapy group.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q3: When the UKPDS results were published in 1998, both patients and clinicians were... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How does the study define the "legacy effect" of intensive glucose control?
items.answer: The legacy effect refers to the phenomenon where the benefits of an early, intensive glucose-control strategy are sustained for years (up to 10 years in this study) despite an early loss of within-trial differences in actual glycemic control.
keyPoints: The legacy effect refers to the phenomenon where the benefits of an early, intensive glucose-control strategy are sustained for years (up to 10 years in this study) despite an early loss of within-trial differences in actual glycemic control.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q4: The legacy effect refers to the phenomenon where the benefits of an early, inten... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why might the accumulation of "advanced glycation end products" explain the biological mechanism behind the legacy effect?
items.answer: Long-term hyperglycemia causes the gradual accumulation of advanced glycation end products, which are only slowly degraded once intensive glycemic control is achieved. Early intensive control prevents their initial formation, potentially protecting the microvasculature and macrovasculature for years afterward.
keyPoints: Long-term hyperglycemia causes the gradual accumulation of advanced glycation end products, which are only slowly degraded once intensive glycemic control is achieved. Early intensive control prevents their initial formation, potentially protecting the microvasculature and macrovasculature for years afterward.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q5: Long-term hyperglycemia causes the gradual accumulation of advanced glycation en... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How did the outcomes of the intensive sulfonylurea-insulin group change over the 10-year follow-up compared to the original trial?
items.answer: While the original trial only showed a nonsignificant 16% risk reduction for myocardial infarction, the 10-year follow-up saw clinically relevant risk reductions successfully emerge for myocardial infarction (15%) and death from any cause (13%).
keyPoints: While the original trial only showed a nonsignificant 16% risk reduction for myocardial infarction, the 10-year follow-up saw clinically relevant risk reductions successfully emerge for myocardial infarction (15%) and death from any cause (13%).
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q6: While the original trial only showed a nonsignificant 16% risk reduction for myo... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why was the metformin arm of the trial restricted to a specific demographic of patients?
items.answer: The intensive metformin therapy was specifically evaluated in patients whose body weight was more than 120% of their ideal weight (overweight patients).
keyPoints: The intensive metformin therapy was specifically evaluated in patients whose body weight was more than 120% of their ideal weight (overweight patients).
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q7: The intensive metformin therapy was specifically evaluated in patients whose bod... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How did the study collect data on patients who could no longer attend the physical UKPDS clinics between years 6 and 10?
items.answer: Because funding for clinic visits was not available, researchers used annual questionnaires sent to the patients and their general practitioners to capture possible clinical outcomes and vital status.
keyPoints: Because funding for clinic visits was not available, researchers used annual questionnaires sent to the patients and their general practitioners to capture possible clinical outcomes and vital status.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q8: Because funding for clinic visits was not available, researchers used annual que... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why do the authors suggest that trials like ADVANCE and ACCORD failed to show similar short-term macrovascular benefits?
items.answer: The ADVANCE and ACCORD trials involved patients who were 8 to 12 years older and had already been treated for diabetes for 8 to 10 years at randomization. The UKPDS focused on newly diagnosed patients, suggesting that cardiovascular benefits require early intervention.
keyPoints: The ADVANCE and ACCORD trials involved patients who were 8 to 12 years older and had already been treated for diabetes for 8 to 10 years at randomization. The UKPDS focused on newly diagnosed patients, suggesting that cardiovascular benefits require early intervention.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q9: The ADVANCE and ACCORD trials involved patients who were 8 to 12 years older and... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How was the objectivity of the clinical outcomes guaranteed during the post-trial phase?
items.answer: Full documentation for all putative outcomes was obtained, and members of the UKPDS end-point committee adjudicated the outcomes while remaining completely unaware of the patients' original assignments to study groups.
keyPoints: Full documentation for all putative outcomes was obtained, and members of the UKPDS end-point committee adjudicated the outcomes while remaining completely unaware of the patients' original assignments to study groups.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q10: Full documentation for all putative outcomes was obtained, and members of the UK... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why was stroke not considered a success for intensive glucose control in this study?
items.answer: Throughout both the original interventional trial and the 10-year post-trial follow-up, no significant risk reductions were observed for fatal or nonfatal stroke in either the sulfonylurea-insulin or metformin groups.
keyPoints: Throughout both the original interventional trial and the 10-year post-trial follow-up, no significant risk reductions were observed for fatal or nonfatal stroke in either the sulfonylurea-insulin or metformin groups.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q11: Throughout both the original interventional trial and the 10-year post-trial fol... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How did the EDIC study in type 1 diabetes provide precedent for the UKPDS findings?
items.answer: The EDIC study followed patients from the DCCT trial and showed that prior intensive insulin therapy significantly reduced the risk of cardiovascular events by 42% long after the trial ended, proving that a legacy effect exists in type 1 diabetes as well.
keyPoints: The EDIC study followed patients from the DCCT trial and showed that prior intensive insulin therapy significantly reduced the risk of cardiovascular events by 42% long after the trial ended, proving that a legacy effect exists in type 1 diabetes as well.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q12: The EDIC study followed patients from the DCCT trial and showed that prior inten... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why did plasma creatinine levels differ slightly between the metformin and conventional groups during post-trial monitoring?
items.answer: Plasma creatinine levels in the metformin group were found to be 15% higher on average than those in the conventional-therapy group, representing the only significant biochemical difference noted across the groups.
keyPoints: Plasma creatinine levels in the metformin group were found to be 15% higher on average than those in the conventional-therapy group, representing the only significant biochemical difference noted across the groups.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q13: Plasma creatinine levels in the metformin group were found to be 15% higher on a... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How did the overall mortality rates highlight the severity of the disease over the study's lifetime?
items.answer: With a median follow-up approaching 17 years, overall mortality was 44.0%, with the leading causes of death being cardiovascular disease (51.5%) and cancer (24.2%).
keyPoints: With a median follow-up approaching 17 years, overall mortality was 44.0%, with the leading causes of death being cardiovascular disease (51.5%) and cancer (24.2%).
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q14: With a median follow-up approaching 17 years, overall mortality was 44.0%, with ... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Why is the legacy effect expected to diminish only slowly over time?
items.answer: While the conventional-therapy group experienced improved glycemic control after the trial, there is a lag phase before that improvement translates into reduced clinical events; simultaneously, the structural biological benefits of early control in the intensive group degrade very slowly.
keyPoints: While the conventional-therapy group experienced improved glycemic control after the trial, there is a lag phase before that improvement translates into reduced clinical events; simultaneously, the structural biological benefits of early control in the intensive group degrade very slowly.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 Q15: While the conventional-therapy group experienced improved glycemic control after... — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: What was the median follow-up period in person-years for the sulfonylurea-insulin group by the end of the post-trial monitoring?
items.explanation: Median follow-up was 16.8 person-years for the sulfonylurea-insulin group.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 1: Median follow-up was 16.8 person-years for the sulfonylurea-insulin group. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: By what time point did the differences in mean glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) levels between the intensive and conventional therapy groups disappear?
items.explanation: HbA1c differences disappeared within the first year of post-trial follow-up.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 2: HbA1c differences disappeared within the first year of post-trial follow-up. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: What was the persistent relative risk reduction for microvascular disease in the sulfonylurea-insulin group at the 10-year post-trial mark?
items.explanation: Microvascular disease RR reduction remained 24% at 10 years.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 3: Microvascular disease RR reduction remained 24% at 10 years. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Which of the following was NOT an original exclusion criterion for the UKPDS?
options: Fasting plasma glucose level of more than 108 mg per deciliter
Myocardial infarction in the previous year
Severe concurrent illness limiting life expectancy
items.explanation: FPG >108 mg/dL was an inclusion criterion, not an exclusion.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 4: FPG >108 mg/dL was an inclusion criterion, not an exclusion. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: What was the relative risk reduction for myocardial infarction in the intensive metformin group at 10 years?
items.explanation: Metformin group had 33% RR reduction for MI at 10 years.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 5: Metformin group had 33% RR reduction for MI at 10 years. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: In the post-trial monitoring phase, what percentage of surviving patients attended the UKPDS clinics during years 1 to 5?
items.explanation: 92–97% attended clinics during years 1–5.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 6: 92–97% attended clinics during years 1–5. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Which two leading causes of death accounted for the majority of the 44.0% overall mortality rate?
options: Cancer and sudden death from hypoglycemia
items.explanation: CV disease (51.5%) and cancer (24.2%) were leading causes of death.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 7: CV disease (51.5%) and cancer (24.2%) were leading causes of death. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: How was the vital status obtained for all patients still living in the United Kingdom?
options: The European Quality of Life-5 Dimensions questionnaire
items.explanation: Vital status obtained via the UK Office of National Statistics.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 8: Vital status obtained via the UK Office of National Statistics. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: In the Steno-2 study mentioned in the discussion, what was the intervention that led to postinterventional mortality benefits?
options: A multifactorial risk-reduction program with multiple drugs and behavior modification
Exclusive use of external insulin pumps
items.explanation: Steno-2 used multifactorial risk reduction with drugs and behavior modification.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 9: Steno-2 used multifactorial risk reduction with drugs and behavior modification. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: At the 5-year mark post-trial, what percentage of the patient cohort was being managed on diet alone?
items.explanation: Only ~5% were on diet alone at 5 years post-trial.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 10: Only ~5% were on diet alone at 5 years post-trial. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: According to the data, which of the following aggregate outcomes did NOT see a significant risk reduction in either the SU-insulin or metformin groups?
items.explanation: No significant stroke risk reduction in either group.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 11: No significant stroke risk reduction in either group. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: What was the primary difference in baseline characteristics between patients who had final-year data available versus those who did not?
options: They had significantly higher body-mass indexes
They were significantly more likely to be on insulin
They were 2 years older and less likely to be white
They had much higher baseline blood pressure
items.explanation: Patients with final-year data were 2 years older and less likely to be white.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 12: Patients with final-year data were 2 years older and less likely to be white. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: What happened to mean body weight differences between the intensive and conventional groups over the post-trial period?
options: The intensive group lost significantly more weight
The conventional group lost significantly more weight
Mean body weight did not differ at baseline or thereafter
Both groups experienced extreme weight gain due to insulin use
items.explanation: Mean body weight did not differ between groups at baseline or thereafter.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 13: Mean body weight did not differ between groups at baseline or thereafter. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: The risk reduction for death from any cause in the sulfonylurea-insulin group by the end of the post-trial follow-up was:
items.explanation: All-cause mortality RR reduction was 13% in the SU-insulin group.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 14: All-cause mortality RR reduction was 13% in the SU-insulin group. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: Which trial was stopped early after 3.5 years due to an unexplained excess rate of death from any cause in its intensive-control group?
items.explanation: ACCORD stopped intensive glycemic regimen early for excess mortality.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 15: ACCORD stopped intensive glycemic regimen early for excess mortality. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: The original UKPDS focused on patients who were newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. What was the average duration of diabetes for patients entering the ADVANCE and ACCORD trials?
items.explanation: ADVANCE/ACCORD patients had diabetes ~8–10 years at randomization.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 16: ADVANCE/ACCORD patients had diabetes ~8–10 years at randomization. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: What limitation was explicitly mentioned regarding the post-trial monitoring data collected between years 6 and 10?
options: The data was falsified by general practitioners
No biochemical or clinical measurements were collected, preventing time-dependent covariate analysis
The Office of National Statistics lost the vital status records
Patients were intentionally placed back on randomized therapies
items.explanation: Years 6–10 lacked biochemical/clinical measurements for covariate analysis.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 17: Years 6–10 lacked biochemical/clinical measurements for covariate analysis. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: What percentage of the overall patient cohort was lost to follow-up over the post-trial period?
items.explanation: Only 3.5% were lost to follow-up.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 18: Only 3.5% were lost to follow-up. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: In the metformin group, what was the risk reduction for any diabetes-related end point at the 10-year mark?
items.explanation: Metformin group had 21% RR reduction for any diabetes-related endpoint.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 19: Metformin group had 21% RR reduction for any diabetes-related endpoint. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.question: To be eligible for the intensive metformin group during the original randomization, a patient's body weight had to be at least what percentage of their ideal weight?
items.explanation: Metformin arm required body weight >120% of ideal weight.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 MCQ 20: Metformin arm required body weight >120% of ideal weight. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: During the 10-year post-trial follow-up, doctors actively tried to maintain the specific glucose-control therapies that patients were assigned to during the original UKPDS.
items.explanation: No attempt was made to maintain original randomized therapies.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 1: No attempt was made to maintain original randomized therapies. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: The "legacy effect" refers to the observation that early, intensive glycemic control yields long-lasting cardiovascular benefits even after strict glycemic differences are lost.
items.explanation: Legacy effect = enduring benefit despite loss of glycemic separation.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 2: Legacy effect = enduring benefit despite loss of glycemic separation. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: By year 5 of the post-trial follow-up, almost 50% of the patient cohort was receiving insulin therapy (with or without oral therapy).
items.explanation: ~49% on insulin (with or without oral agents) at year 5.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 3: ~49% on insulin (with or without oral agents) at year 5. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: The intensive sulfonylurea-insulin group experienced a statistically significant reduction in the risk of stroke during the post-trial period.
items.explanation: No significant stroke reduction in SU-insulin or metformin groups.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 4: No significant stroke reduction in SU-insulin or metformin groups. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: The ADVANCE and ACCORD trials both successfully demonstrated that aggressive glucose control rapidly reduces macrovascular events in older patients with long-standing diabetes.
items.explanation: ADVANCE/ACCORD did not show rapid macrovascular benefit like early UKPDS legacy.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 5: ADVANCE/ACCORD did not show rapid macrovascular benefit like early UKPDS legacy. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: At the end of the post-trial monitoring period, the intensive sulfonylurea-insulin group still maintained a significantly lower HbA1c than the conventional-therapy group.
items.explanation: Glycemic differences disappeared within 1 year post-trial.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 6: Glycemic differences disappeared within 1 year post-trial. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: The Steno-2 study provided additional evidence supporting the concept of postinterventional benefits in patients with type 2 diabetes.
items.explanation: Steno-2 showed postinterventional mortality benefits with multifactorial care.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 7: Steno-2 showed postinterventional mortality benefits with multifactorial care. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: Cancer was the single leading cause of death in the UKPDS survivor cohort, accounting for over 50% of all mortality.
items.explanation: CV disease was leading cause (51.5%); cancer was 24.2%.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 8: CV disease was leading cause (51.5%); cancer was 24.2%. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: The UKPDS end-point committee adjudicated clinical outcomes based on hospital records while remaining completely blinded to the patients' original randomized treatment groups.
items.explanation: Endpoint committee adjudicated outcomes blinded to original assignment.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 9: Endpoint committee adjudicated outcomes blinded to original assignment. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: For the sulfonylurea-insulin group, the reduction in the risk of myocardial infarction was significant during the original interventional trial, but the significance was lost during the post-trial follow-up.
items.explanation: MI reduction was nonsignificant during trial but became significant at 10 years.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 10: MI reduction was nonsignificant during trial but became significant at 10 years. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: In the intensive metformin group, the 10-year post-trial risk reduction for death from any cause was 27%.
items.explanation: Metformin group: 27% RR reduction for all-cause death at 10 years.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 11: Metformin group: 27% RR reduction for all-cause death at 10 years. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: Blood pressure levels differed significantly between the intensive and conventional therapy groups throughout the post-trial period.
items.explanation: Blood pressure did not differ significantly between groups post-trial.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 12: Blood pressure did not differ significantly between groups post-trial. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: The accumulation of advanced glycation end products is proposed by the authors as a possible biological mechanism to explain why microvascular disease occurs slowly and why the legacy effect exists.
items.explanation: AGE accumulation proposed as mechanism for slow microvascular harm and legacy effect.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 13: AGE accumulation proposed as mechanism for slow microvascular harm and legacy effect. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: The post-trial monitoring phase was entirely funded by pharmaceutical companies from the day the interventional trial ended.
items.explanation: Clinic funding ended; years 6–10 relied on questionnaires, not industry-only funding.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 14: Clinic funding ended; years 6–10 relied on questionnaires, not industry-only funding. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.

items.statement: Risk reductions for peripheral vascular disease were not statistically significant in either the sulfonylurea-insulin group or the metformin group at the end of the 10-year follow-up.
items.explanation: Peripheral vascular disease reductions were not statistically significant.
items.reference: UKPDS 80 T/F 15: Peripheral vascular disease reductions were not statistically significant. — Holman RR, Paul SK, Bethel MA, Matthews DR, Neil HA. 10-year follow-up of intensive glucose control in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2008;359:1577-1587.
